content.text: Permissions are a first-class concern in Expo apps that call Claude from a mobile device. Camera, microphone, location, and notification access must be explicitly granted by the user — and your app must handle every possible state gracefully. Misstep here and users see system dialogs with no context, permanently deny access, or your Claude feature silently fails. This section covers Expo's unified permissions API, the check-request-rationale pattern, platform-specific edge cases, and a reusable hook you can drop into any multi-model Expo project.
Every sensitive capability in Expo is guarded by a package-level permissions API. Each package (
to read the current state without triggering a dialog, and

attrs.alt: Permission request decision flow: Feature Triggered → getPermissionsAsync → granted uses feature, undetermined shows rationale → requestPermissionsAsync → granted uses feature, denied shows Linking.openSettings()

Each Expo package requires its own app.json plugin entry, which injects the required native strings into Info.plist (iOS) and AndroidManifest.xml (Android).

The string values in these plugin options are user-facing. iOS in particular requires strings that explain clearly why your app needs the capability — App Review rejects vague strings like "This app needs camera access."
iOS 14+ added a second dimension to location permission: full vs. reduced accuracy. Request
unless you need GPS-precise coordinates. Claude features typically only need city-level context, making

Rather than copying the check/request/rationale logic into every screen, encapsulate it in a hook. The hook returns the current status, a function to request permission, and a flag for showing a settings prompt.
Granted by default on older API; required on Android 13+
Notification API; push requires service worker
GPS, Network, or IP; no explicit tier

Push notifications for async Claude agent results (covered in the previous section) require a separate permission flow. Unlike camera or microphone, notification permission on iOS can only be requested once — the system does not allow re-prompting after a deny. On Android 13+ (API level 33) it must also be explicitly requested.
Before API level 33, Android granted notification permission automatically. Starting with SDK 46, you must call
in your app.json permissions array if targeting API 33+.
Showing a rationale before the system dialog dramatically improves grant rates. There are two effective patterns depending on the context:

Use an Alert dialog just before requesting. This is the lowest friction approach and works for any permission type. The key is specificity — link the permission to the Claude feature that depends on it:

For high-value features, render an inline card in the screen UI that explains the capability and has a "Enable" button. This lets users understand the feature without a modal interruption and is appropriate for features shown at app launch.
Permissions can change while your app is backgrounded — the user can revoke access in Settings at any time. Never cache permission status across app sessions. Instead, call
every time the feature is about to be used, or use the
API to re-check when the app becomes active:
Permissions are difficult to test in simulators and emulators because the OS dialogs behave differently. Use these strategies:
Mock the Expo permission modules to return each status state and verify your hook handles all three branches:
iOS Simulator: Device > Privacy > Camera. Android emulator: Settings > Apps > [Your App] > Permissions. Revoke and re-grant between test runs.

Always test the full permission flow on real devices with internal distribution before submitting to stores. Simulator behavior diverges from real-device behavior for microphone, camera, and notifications.
on iOS, calling request again does nothing — no dialog appears, no status change. Always check current status first.
Missing the plugin entry in app.json.
Without the plugin, the Info.plist usage strings are absent. iOS 14+ throws an exception at runtime when your code tries to access camera/microphone/location without the usage string.
Assuming notifications are free on Android.
Android 13+ (API 33+, Expo SDK 48+) requires explicit notification permission. If your minimum targetSdkVersion is 33 or above and you skip the request, silent pushes are never shown.
Caching permission state across app sessions.
A user can revoke permissions in Settings at any time.

No fallback when permission is blocked.
If your only response to a permanent deny is silently disabling a button, users won't understand why the feature doesn't work. Always surface a "Go to Settings" CTA when
Robust permission handling requires four things: the right app.json plugin configuration to register native capability strings, the check-before-request pattern using
, a specific rationale shown to the user before the OS dialog, and a graceful fallback to
when permission is permanently denied. Encapsulate this logic in a

hook that accepts the getter, requester, and rationale strings — then compose it for camera, microphone, location, and notifications without duplicating the state machine. Re-check permissions on every app foreground event to stay in sync with what the user has set in the OS Settings app.
